title: Setting Default Data Types as Parameters

content.problemStatement:
In C++, you can provide default data types as template parameters to generic classes by specifying default template arguments. Default template arguments allow you to define a default data type for a template parameter, which can be overridden when needed. Here's how you can use default data types as parameters for generic classes:

In this example:

1. We define a generic class template `Container` with a default template argument `T = int`. This means that if you don't specify a data type when creating an instance of `Container`, it will default to `int`.

2. In the constructor of the `Container` class, we initialize the `data` member with the value passed as an argument.

3. The `getValue` member function retrieves and returns the value stored in the `data` member.

4. In the `main` function, we create instances of the `Container` class with different data types:
   - `Container<> intContainer(42)` uses the default data type (`int`).
   - `Container doubleContainer(3.14)` specifies `double` as the data type.
   - `Container stringContainer("Hello, C++!")` specifies `std::string` as the data type.

By using default data types as template parameters, you can provide flexibility in your generic class while also specifying defaults for cases where the data type is not explicitly specified.
constraints:
- $1 \leq T \leq 100$
- $1 \leq N \leq 100$
- $1 \leq M \leq N\cdot(N-1)/2$
- $1 \leq u_i, v_i \leq N$
- $u_i \neq v_i$ for each $1 \leq i \leq M$.
- The sum of $N$ over all test cases won't exceed $100$.
content.inputFormat:
- The first line of input will contain a single integer $T$, denoting the number of test cases.
- Each test case consists of multiple lines of input.
    - The first line of each test case contains two space-separated integers $N$ and $M$ — the number of vertices and edges of the graph, respectively.
    - The next $M$ lines describe the edges. The $i$-th of these $M$ lines contains two space-separated integers $u_i$ and $v_i$, denoting an edge between $u_i$ and $v_i$.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output on a new line the number of good subgraphs of $G$, modulo $10^9 + 7$.
